# Andrew O'Malley (Medicine)

<!-- vimeo: 1111480831 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Hello, I'm Andrew O'Malley, a senior lecturer at the University of St. Andrews School of Medicine. I lead our division of education and we deliver several medical programs that train doctors for a wide range of practice settings from large urban hospitals to remote and rural communities. A key part of these programs is developing students' ability to communicate effectively with patients, but across the UK medical school places will double by 2031 while placement capacity and role-play actors remain static. To address this challenge, my team has developed a patient simulator. It's an AI powered platform that allows students to rehearse full consultations in three formats, a text chat for quick accessible practice voice calls with realistic speech synthesis and transcription, and full motion live video avatars showing nuanced facial expressions. After each session, students receive instant structured feedback on five domains of communication from opening the consultation to closing it mapped to the Calgary Cambridge framework and the UK Medical Licensing Assessment. Educators can create their own cases and virtual patients to match their curricula, ensuring that our system aligns with their local learning objectives. Students can also generate a random patient or select from a list of cases covering a broad range of presenting complaints. Underpinning this is our patient generator, which contains a diversity engine to ensure that patients appropriately represent groups that are often underrepresented in medical training, varying by age, ethnicity, accent, literacy level, and socioeconomic context. This not only improves representation, but strengthens students' ability to practice culturally competent care. Technically. Our patient simulator integrates three open AI models. We have a fine tuned GPT-4 oh mini for real time patient dialogue, trained on over 60 hours of anonymized consultation transcripts from our medical school. We use GPT-4 0.1 for structured reasoning based feedback in Jason format, and we use whisper one for speech to text in audio and video consultations. The fine tuning was critical. Generic LLMs can be fluent, but lack clinical authenticity. Our dataset captured the pacing, empathy and questioning style of skilled clinicians. The platform is a full stack cloud application. It's got a scalable backend to support thousands of concurrent users. It's modular so we can customize our case libraries and it uses secure APIs for integration with other platforms. We're currently evaluating our patient simulator through a randomized control trial with 40 students and a much larger rollout to around 700 students is planned for next year. While formal analysis is still to come early use has shown strong engagement. Students are exploring multiple scenarios and trying different approaches to consultations. Educators report that our patient simulator helps provide more rehearsal opportunities without the logistical challenges of scheduling actors. We've also seen students use the system outside of scheduled teaching time, suggesting that it's accessible and motivating for self-directed learning. Over the coming months, we'll analyze the data to understand the platform's effect on skills, confidence, and preparedness, and gather structured feedback from students and faculty to guide refinements. In parallel, we're in discussions with other universities, ed tech companies and academic publishers about integrating our patient simulator into their existing offerings, extending its reach beyond our own medical school. For me, the innovation isn't just in the AI models. It's in applying them to solve a systemic training capacity problem while keeping pedagogy and diversity at the heart of the design. With open AI's tools, we've gone from concept to fully functional simulator in under a year, and our students are already seeing the benefits. Thank you for listening. I look forward to learning from the other contributions to the AI Academy.
